coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent carbon quantum dots-based prodrug nanosponges with outstanding tumor-specific drug delivery and imaging
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Society of Powder Technology Japan. Published by Elsevier B.V. and The Society of Powder Technology Japan. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Society of Powder Technology Japan. Published by Elsevier B.V. and The Society of Powder Technology Japan.
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Wei
Liu, Peng
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQD)-based nanosponges have been widely designed for tumor theranostic application recently. However, the drug content is usually lower with the shorter dynamic covalent linkers while the longer hydrophilic dynamic covalent linkers led to a significant premature drug leakage. Here, a hydrophobic linear reducible-responsive linker with two terminal aldehyde group was designed to crosslink the hydrazine-functionalized PEGylated CQDs (Hy-CQD-PEG), resulting in Hy-CQDss-PEG nanosponges. After doxorubicin (DOX) conjugation, the DOX-Hy-CQDss-PEG prodrug nanosponges were obtained with DOX content of 18.76 % and hydrodynamic diameter of 140 nm, respectively. The DOX-Hy-CQDss-PEG nanosponges were stable in the normal physiological medium with very low drug leakage and fluorescence, while they could disintegrate in the tumor intracellular microenvironment, releasing DOX and the hydrazine-functionalized CQDs for theranostic application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tumor theranostics
Prodrug nanosponges
pH/reduction dual-triggered
Fluorescence imaging
Hydrophobic linker

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of DOX-Hy-CQDss-PEG prodrug nanosponges
Cellular uptake and in vitro cytotoxicity

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: 1H NMR spectrum of the hydrophobic linear reducible-responsive linker.

Hydrodynamic diameters and distributions of CQD (a), Hy-CQD-PEG (b), Hy-CQDss-PEG-1:3 (c) in aqueous dispersion; DOX-Hy-CQDss-PEG in aqueous dispersion for 0 h (d) and 7 days (e); and DOX-Hy-CQDss-PEG in pH 7.4 PBS + 10 μM GSH for 12 h (f).
TEM images of CQD (a), Hy-CQD-PEG (b), Hy-CQDss-PEG-1:3 (c) and DOX-Hy-CQDss-PEG (d).

Cumulative drug release profiles from the DOX-Hy-CQDss-PEG in different releasing media (a) and cytotoxicity of Hy-CQDss-PEG, DOX-Hy-CQDss-PEG and DOX (b). The statistical significance was set at *p < 0.05.

Fluorescence emission spectra of CQD (a), Hy-CQD-PEG (b), Hy-CQDss-PEG-1:3 (c) and DOX-Hy-CQDss-PEG (d) in aqueous dispersions, DOX- Hy-CQDss-PEG (0.05 mg/mL) in pH 5.0 ABS for different times at λex = 360 nm (e). Inset: digital images of the dispersions under 365 nm UV (right) and daylight (left).

CLSM images of HepG 2 cells after incubation with DOX-Hy-CQDss-PEG. The label a, b and c represent the images of the channels of DAPI, DOX and CQD, the d is the merged images of a with b, the e is the merged images of a with c, the f is the merged images of b with c.
Fabrication of pH/reduction dual-responsive prodrug nanosponges.
Synthesis of the hydrophobic linear reducible-responsive linker.
Fitted parameters with the Higuchi and Korsmeyer–Peppas models.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent carbon quantum dots-based prodrug nanosponges with outstanding tumor-specific drug delivery and imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qinghai Normal University, Xining 810016, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qinghai Normal University, Xining 810016, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQD)-based nanosponges have been widely designed for tumor theranostic application recently. However, the drug content is usually lower with the shorter dynamic covalent linkers while the longer hydrophilic dynamic covalent linkers led to a significant premature drug leakage. Here, a hydrophobic linear reducible-responsive linker with two terminal aldehyde group was designed to crosslink the hydrazine-functionalized PEGylated CQDs (Hy-CQD-PEG), resulting in Hy-CQDss-PEG nanosponges. After doxorubicin (DOX) conjugation, the DOX-Hy-CQDss-PEG prodrug nanosponges were obtained with DOX content of 18.76 % and hydrodynamic diameter of 140 nm, respectively. The DOX-Hy-CQDss-PEG nanosponges were stable in the normal physiological medium with very low drug leakage and fluorescence, while they could disintegrate in the tumor intracellular microenvironment, releasing DOX and the hydrazine-functionalized CQDs for theranostic application.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted intense interests in biomedical field owing to their excellent biocompatibility and fluorescent property [1]. But their practical application in tumor fluorescence imaging is restricted by their very small size, making them quickly cleared by the kidneys. Besides the CQDs-containing nanocomposites in which the CQDs’ content was usually too low [2–6], the CQDs-based nanotheranostics have been widely reported with a higher CQDs’ content via the non-covalent self-assembly [7–11] or covalent self-assembly of the modified CQDs [12–18], in order to avoid the rapid renal clearance.

Comparatively, the CQDs-based nanotheranostics via the non-covalent self-assembly could easily disintegrate into CQDs for a better fluorescence recovery after release the conjugated hydrophobic chemotherapeutics, however, the stability of such non-covalent nanoassemblies is not so ideal as desired, especially the extreme dilution during the intravenous injection. As for the ones via stable self-assembly, the CQDs-containing crosslinked frameworks were remained after drug release [12–14], declining the fluorescent property of CQDs. To solve the problem, various dynamic covalent bonds, such as acid-labile ones [15,16] and reducible cleavable ones [17,18], have been used to fabricate the CQDs-based nanotheranostics for the aim of pH- or GSH-triggered disintegration.

Comparing the CQDs-based nanotheranostics via dynamic covalent bonds, it could be concluded that the shorter linkers between the building blocks of CQDs led to a lower drug contents due to the space hindrance, while the longer hydrophilic linkers resulted in a non-ignorable premature drug leakage the simulated normal physiological medium, such as blood circulation. Based on the above challenge, a hydrophobic linear reducible-responsive linker with two terminal aldehyde group was designed to fabricate the CQD-based nanosponges via acid-labile conjugation in the present work. After DOX conjugation via acid-labile bond, the fluorescent carbon quantum dots-based prodrug nanosponges were obtained (Scheme 1 ). Their outstanding tumor-specific drug delivery and imaging property were revealed for the future tumor theranostic application.

Carboxyl-monomethoxy poly(ethylene glycol) (mPEG-COOH, Mn = 2000) was purchased from Beijing Kaiping Biotech Co. ltd. Bis(2-hydroxyethyl) disulfide was bought Shanghai Deepak Biotechnology Co., ltd. Hexamethylene diisocyanate (HDI, 99 %) were purchased from Aladdin Chem. Co., ltd. Hydrazine hydrate (N2H4·H2O, >80.0 %) was bought from Tianjin Guangfu Fine Chemical Research Institute. p-Hydroxybenzaldehyde (99 %) was obtained from Heowns Biochemical Technology Co., ltd. Doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX·HCl, 99.4 %) was obtained from Beijing Huafeng United Technol. Co., ltd. All other reagents and solvents were analytical grade.

2.0 mmol of HDI was dissolved in 10 mL DMF, and then 1.0 mmol of bis(2-hydroxyethyl) disulfide was slowly dropped into the solution within 2 h with stirring in N2 atmosphere. After the mixture was stirred at 85 °C for 3 h, 2.0 mmol of p-hydroxybenzaldehyde was added and the mixture was stirred at 85 °C for further 3 h (Scheme 2 ). The product was collected by precipitation with ethanol, as yellow solid.

The Hy-CQD-PEG was synthesized by hydrazine-functionalization with excess hydrazine and PEGylation of nitrogen-doped CQDs (Scheme 3 ) [11], which were prepared with the microwave-assisted pyrolysis of citric acid and ethylenediamine.

20.0 mg of Hy-CQD-PEG was dispersed into 15 mL of DMSO. The functional linker was added into the dispersion with Hy-CQD-PEG/linker mass ratio of 1:1, 1:2, 1:3, 1:4 or 1:5, respectively. After adding 2 drops of glacial acetic acid, the mixture was stirred for 24 h at room temperature in the dark. The light yellow Hy-CQDss-PEG nanosponges were collected by dialyzing the resultant dispersion against water for 5 days (MW = 8000), named Hy-CQDss-PEG-1:1, Hy-CQDss-PEG-1:2, Hy-CQDss-PEG-1:3, Hy-CQDss-PEG-1:4 and Hy-CQDss-PEG-1:5 respectively.

40.0 mg of Hy-CQDss-PEG nanosponges were dispersed into 20 mL of DMSO. Then DOX (40.0 mg), trifluoroacetic acid (40 μL) and trimethylamine (400 μL) were added. After the mixture was stirred at room temperature for 24 h in dark, the nanosponges were collected by precipitation with ethanol. After three cycles of dispersion in DMSO-precipitation in ethanol to completely remove the excess DOX, the DOX-Hy-CQDss-PEG prodrug nanosponges were obtained as dark red powder.

The DOX release performance was assessed in vitro in the buffer solutions with different pH values and GSH levels. Typically, 4.0 mg of DOX-Hy-CQDss-PEG prodrug nanosponges was dispersed into 10 mL of buffer solution. The dispersion was transferred to a dialysis bag (MW = 1000 Da) and placed in 110 mL of corresponding solution. After shaking at 37℃ for certain time, 5.0 mL of dialysate was taken out to measure DOX concentration with UV/vis spectrometry (LOD of 5.8 × 10-2, 3.8 × 10-2, 8.7 × 10-2 and 3.4 × 10-2 μg/mL in pH 7.4, pH 7.4 + 10 μM GSH, pH 5.0 and pH 5.0 + 10 mM GSH respectively at Signal-to-Noise ratio of 3 with RSD ≤ 3 % and accuracy ≥ 95 %, Fig. S1). Meantime, the same volume of fresh buffer solution was added in the dialysate.

MTT assay was used to in vitro evaluate the toxicity of prodrug nanosponges with the human hepatoma cells (HepG2) and human hepatocytes cells (L02), respectively. Fter the cells were cultivated on 96-well plates (1 × 105 cells per well) in an incubator with atmospheric humidity (5 % CO2, 37 ℃) for 24 h, the Hy-CQDss-PEG, DOX-Hy-CQDss-PEG or DOX was added with different concentrations, followed with incubation for 48 h. Then, 20 μL (5 mg/mL) of MTT solution was added to each well for further incubation of 4 h. The MTT solution in the hole was sucked out and washed with PBS for 3 times. Finally, the absorbance of the solution was measured at 490 nm with a microplate analyzer to detect the cell viability.

CLSM technique was used to study the endocytosis of the DOX-Hy-CQDss-PEG prodrug nanosponges by the HepG2 cells. The cells were cultivated on 24-well plates (1 × 105 cells per well) in an incubator with atmospheric humidity (5 % CO2, 37 ℃) for 12 h. And then 100 μL of DOX-Hy-CQDss-PEG dispersion (DOX equivalent concentration of 15 μg/mL) were added, following with incubation for 48 h. After washing with culture solution, the cells were dyed with DAPI and fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde, and the inverted fluorescence microscope (Olympus, IX 71) was used to observe the fluorescence distribution in the cells.

1H NMR analysis was conducted on a 400 MHz NMR spectrometer (JEOL), with DMSO‑d 6 as the solvent. BI-200SM dynamic light scattering was used to measure the hydrodynamic diameter (Dh) and distribution of the samples in aqueous dispersions at 25℃. The morphology of the samples was characterized by JEM-1200EX transmission electron microscope (TEM), sampling with their aqueous dispersion. UV adsorption spectra of the samples were recorded on a UV/vis Spectrometer (TU-1901) with the concentration of 0.05 mg/mL in aqueous dispersions and DOX concentration was measured at 480 nm at room temperature. Fluorescent emission spectra of the CQD, Hy-CQD-PEG, Hy-CQDss-PEG-1:3 and DOX-Hy-CQDss-PEG were recorded on a Hitachi F-7500 fluorescence spectrometer, with their aqueous dispersion with the solid content of 0.05 mg/mL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of DOX-Hy-CQDss-PEG prodrug nanosponges
ce:section.ce:para: In the present work, a hydrophobic linear reducible-responsive linker with two terminal aldehyde group was designed to minimize the premature drug leakage of the CQD-based prodrug nanosponges by enhancing their hydrophobicity (Scheme 2). Its chemical structure was revealed with the 1H NMR analysis (Fig. 1 ). The integral area ratio of the three characteristic protons of h:c:a was found as 0.48:0.90:1.07, near the theoretical value of 1:2:2.

With Hy-CQD-PEG as multifunctional building blocks (Scheme 3) [11], the Hy-CQDss-PEG nanosponges were constructed with different feeding ratios (Scheme 1). Compared with the smaller hydrodynamic diameter with unique distribution of the bare CQDs and Hy-CQD-PEG (Fig. 2 (a,b)), the Hy-CQDss-PEG nanosponges showed much bigger diameter with wide distribution (Fig. S2), except the Hy-CQDss-PEG-1:3 around 90 nm with very narrow distribution (Fig. 2c). So the Hy-CQDss-PEG-1:3 synthesized with the Hy-CQD-PEG/linker mass ratio of 1:3 was selected for the further experiment. The Hy-CQDss-PEG-1:3 nanosponges showed near spherical shape in the range of 20–30 nm from the TEM observation (Fig. 3 c). Compared with the reported work with PEG-containing linker [16], the swelling ratio of the proposed nanosponges was higher, due to the spreading chain of the PEG brushes on the surface of the Hy-CQDss-PEG nanosponges as a shell layer in the DLS analysis.

Finally, the DOX-Hy-CQDss-PEG prodrug nanosponges were constructed by conjugating DOX onto the Hy-CQDss-PEG-1:3 nanosponges via acid-labile bonds in DMSO, in which the Hy-CQDss-PEG-1:3 nanosponges could be swollen well, therefore, the dissolved DOX molecules could enter the swollen nanosponges and conjugate from the amino and hydrazide groups on the CQDs (Scheme 1). After complete removal of the free DOX by washing with DMSO, the DOX-Hy-CQDss-PEG prodrug nanosponges were obtained with DOX content of 18.76 %, determined with the UV/vis method. The drug content was much higher than the reported CQD-based prodrug nanosponges with shorter linkers between the building blocks of CQDs [17]. It revealed that the longer linkers between the building blocks of CQDs could boost the drug content in the CQD-based prodrug nanosponges, as reported previously [14,16].

The DOX-Hy-CQDss-PEG prodrug nanosponges maintained the near spherical shape but their size increased obviously in comparison with the bare Hy-CQDss-PEG-1:3 nanosponges (Fig. 3). In the DLS analysis, the Dh of the prodrug nanosponges increased to 140 nm (Fig. 2d). Furthermore, there was no obvious change in the hydrodynamic diameter distribution in water after 7 days (Fig. 2e), demonstrating their excellent stability. Even treating with pH 7.4 PBS + 10 μM GSH for 12 h, only slight swelling was found with narrow unimodal distribution (Fig. 2f). The constant hydrodynamic diameter distribution in water and simulated normal physiological medium indicated that there was neither aggregation nor disintegration of the prodrug nanosponges during storage and in blood circulation, revealing good stability and dispersibility of the proposed formulation.

The in vitro DOX release was evaluated in different media. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the DOX-Hy-CQDss-PEG prodrug nanosponges showed excellent pH/reduction dual-responsive drug release. The premature DOX leakage was 7 % in the simulated normal physiological medium (pH 7.4 PBS + 10 μM GSH), much lower than the reported ones fabricated with PEG-containing linker [16]. As in the simulated intracellular environment, the DOX release was faster in the tumor cells (pH 5.0 + 10 mM GSH) than the normal cells (pH 5.0 ABS), and the cumulative release reached 86.5 % and 72.8 % within 48 h, without burst release. After 96 h, the cumulative release was 89.8 % and 79.8 %, respectively. Such results indicated that the proposed DOX-Hy-CQDss-PEG prodrug nanosponges could efficiently improve the non-specific release of the free DOX, which could be released completely within few hours in all the media [19]. In particular, the premature DOX leakage of the proposed DOX-Hy-CQDss-PEG prodrug nanosponges in the simulated normal physiological medium was lower than those of the reported CQD-based prodrug nanosponges with longer hydrophilic linkers between the building blocks of CQDs [14,16]. The results demonstrated that the longer hydrophobic linkers between the building blocks of CQDs could not only boost the drug content but also minimize the premature DOX leakage.

To explain the drug release behavior, the morphology and hydrodynamic diameter of the prodrug nanosponges were investigated after drug release in the simulated tumor intracellular microenvironment for different times. Different from the desired disintegration of the prodrug nanosponges by cleaving the acid-labile imine and hydrazone conjugation, as well as the bioreducible disulfide bond, more uniform microspheres were formed during the drug release (Fig. S3). Furthermore, the diameter of the microspheres increased with prolonging the drug releasing time. It could be explained as following: i) the prodrug nanosponges disintegrated into small pieces including DOX, Hy-CQD-PEG and cleaved linker (as half linker by cleaving the middle disulfide bond); ii) DOX was released due to the protonation in the acidic media, while the cleaved linker could not dissolve in such media, therefore, it was self-assembled into microspheres with the Hy-CQD-PEG as hybrid surfactant. Unavoidably, some DOX might be encapsulated into the hydrophobic cores of the formed microspheres, as a result, the DOX could not be completely released. As one can probably imagine, the reducible-responsive linker could not be cleaved in the pH 5.0 ABS mimicking the intracellular environment of the normal cells, accordingly, the drug release was slower, and the cumulative release was lower than in the simulated tumor intracellular microenvironment (pH 5.0 + 10 mM GSH).

The above disintegration and self-assembly behaviors during the pH/reduction dual-triggered DOX release could also be revealed by the DLS analysis (Fig. S4). The hydrodynamic diameter increased, and the diameter distribution became wide during the DOX release, but there was no signal of the Hy-CQD-PEG of about 22 nm (Fig. 2b). The phenomenon demonstrated the self-assembly of the disintegrated product (cleaved linker) with the Hy-CQD-PEG as hybrid surfactant, forming microspheres with increased size by prolonging the treating time (Fig. S3).

The drug release profiles were also fitted with the Higuchi and Korsmeyer-Peppas models to explain the release mechanism (Fig. S5). As summarized in Table 1 , the correlation coefficients from the Higuchi model were higher than those from the Korsmeyer-Peppas one, indicating the drug release from the proposed prodrug nanosponges could be better explained with the Fickian diffusion. Moreover, the k value rose significantly with increasing the media acidity and GSH level, further revealing the pH/reduction dual-triggered DOX release from the proposed prodrug nanosponges. As in the Korsmeyer-Peppas model, the diffusion exponent (n) was higher than 1, indicating a case II transport, including polymer relaxation or erosion [20], as discussed above.

The as-prepared CQD showed strong absorption at 238 and 350 nm, attributed to the π-π* transition of CC bonds and the n-π* transition of CN and CO bonds, respectively (Fig. S6) [21]. After surface functionalization and crosslinking with the hydrophobic linear reducible-responsive linker from their surface carboxylic acid and amino groups, such absorptions became weaker and weaker. Finally, such absorptions disappeared after conjugating DOX. The DOX-Hy-CQDss-PEG prodrug nanosponges showed only the characteristic absorptions of DOX and that at 480 nm shifted to 493 nm.

In the fluorescence analysis, the CQD exhibited wavelength independent fluorescence at 450 nm with maximum excitation wavelength of 360 nm (Fig. 5 a). They could emit bright blue fluorescence under 365 nm ultraviolet lamp (Fig. 5a, inset). After surface functionalization, crosslinking with the hydrophobic linear reducible-responsive linker from their surface carboxylic acid and amino groups, and final conjugating DOX, the fluorescence intensity of the Hy-CQD-PEG, Hy-CQDss-PEG-1:3 and DOX-Hy-CQDss-PEG became weaker and weaker in turn (Fig. 5 b-d). It demonstrated that the fluorescence of CQD could be efficiently quenched in the construction of the DOX-Hy-CQDss-PEG prodrug nanosponges via static quenching [22] and dynamic quenching [23].

In the proposed design concept, the DOX-Hy-CQDss-PEG prodrug nanosponges should be stable in the normal physiological medium without fluorescence but could disintegrate into small pieces in the tumor intracellular microenvironment, releasing DOX and the Hy-CQD-PEG with stronger fluorescence intensity for the chemotherapy and fluorescence imaging diagnosis functions, respectively. So the DOX-Hy-CQDss-PEG prodrug nanosponges were treated in the simulated tumor intracellular microenvironment to assess their fluorescence behavior. As shown in Fig. 5e, the fluorescence emissions of CQD and DOX became stronger and stronger with prolonging the treating time. The design concept has been realized with the fluorescence recovery in the tumor intracellular microenvironment, although the fluorescence intensity did not completely recover as the Hy-CQD-PEG because of the re-assembly of the disintegrated pieces as above discussed (Fig. S4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular uptake and in vitro cytotoxicity
ce:section.ce:para: The cellular uptake and subcellular distribution of the DOX-Hy-CQDss-PEG prodrug nanosponges in the tumor cells were investigated in vitro via the CLSM technique after incubating the HepG2 cells with the DOX-Hy-CQDss-PEG (DOX equivalent concentration of 15 μg/mL) for 48 h. As shown in Fig. 6 , both the red fluorescence of DOX and green fluorescence of CQD could be seen in the HepG2 cells, and both them covered the blue fluorescence of the cell nuclei stained with DAPI. The results indicated that the DOX-Hy-CQDss-PEG prodrug nanosponges could be internalized by the HepG2 cells and disintegrated in the tumor intracellular microenvironment, releasing DOX and the Hy-CQD-PEG. The stronger green fluorescence of CQD was achieved in the real tumor intracellular microenvironment, different from the simulated tumor intracellular microenvironment. It may be due to some components in the cells restricted the re-assembly of the disintegrated pieces with the Hy-CQD-PEG as surfactant. Therefore, the Hy-CQD-PEG could accumulate in cell nuclei because of the strong interaction between CQD and DNA [24].

Finally, the in vitro cytotoxicity of the DOX-Hy-CQDss-PEG prodrug nanosponges was evaluated with the MTT assays. As shown in Fig. 4b, the Hy-CQDss-PEG nanosponges showed excellent cytocompatibility with HepG2 cell viability of above 90 %. As for the DOX-Hy-CQDss-PEG prodrug nanosponges, a dose-dependent toxicity was achieved on the HepG2 cells, similar as free DOX. In addition, the half maximal inhibitory concentration (IC50) of the DOX-Hy-CQDss-PEG and free DOX were calculated as 8.4 μg/mL and 10.4 μg/mL at 48 h respectively, demonstrating the enhanced tumor growth inhibition effect of the proposed DOX-Hy-CQDss-PEG prodrug nanosponges.

In the present work, the two dynamic covalent bonds, acid-labile imine and bioreducible disulfide, were used to construct the pH/reduction dual-responsive nanosponges, in a series-mode (Scheme 1). As a result, either stimulus would trigger the disintegration of the final prodrug nanosponges. It meant that the proposed prodrug nanosponges would disintegrate in the normal cells, causing toxic side effects. To reveal it, the cytotoxicity of the proposed prodrug nanosponges on the human normal liver cell line (L02) was also evaluated, in comparison with free DOX. It was found that the proposed DOX-Hy-CQDss-PEG prodrug nanosponges showed much lower cytotoxicity than free DOX, meaning lower toxic side effects on the normal cells because of their slower drug releasing rate because of the lower GSH level. Such results demonstrated the tumor-specific disintegration of the proposed prodrug nanosponges for effective chemotherapy with low toxic side effects.

In summary, novel hydrophobic linear reducible-responsive linker with two terminal aldehyde group was designed to fabricate the CQD-based nanosponges via acid-labile conjugation. After DOX conjugation via acid-labile bond, the fluorescent carbon quantum dots-based DOX-Hy-CQDss-PEG prodrug nanosponges were obtained with excellent pH/reduction dual-triggered disintegration, releasing DOX and the Hy-CQD-PEG for the chemotherapy and fluorescence imaging diagnosis functions, respectively. The hydrophobic linker endowed a very low premature drug leakage from the proposed DOX-Hy-CQDss-PEG in the normal physiological medium while its disulfide bond provided the tumor-selective fast drug release. As a result, the enhanced tumor growth inhibition effect was achieved with the proposed DOX-Hy-CQDss-PEG prodrug nanosponges than free DOX, but lower toxic side effects on the normal cells. Such features make the proposed DOX-Hy-CQDss-PEG prodrug nanosponges a promising nanotheranostics for future tumor treatment.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Gansu Province, China (Grant No. 18JR3RA271).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apt.2022.103816.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

Chin. Opt.

Biopharm. 117 (2017) 105–115.

Carbohydr. Polym.
